attractions.name: 167 Raw Oyster Bar
attractions.description: 167 Raw is a New England-style Oyster Bar located in the heart of historic downtown Charleston. The menu highlights the East Coast's seafood bounty, offering a fresh and vibrant dining experience focused on quality and atmosphere (Official Website).
attractions.hours: Mon - Sat: 11:00 AM - 11:00 PM; Closed Sundays (Official Website)
attractions.admission: Free (Official Website)
highlights: 'Fresh East Coast oysters and curated raw bar samplers ([The Charleston Guide)', 'Signature New England-style lobster rolls and tuna burgers (The Stripe)', 'Acclaimed fish and shrimp tacos voted among the best in the city (The Charleston Guide)', 'Vibrant, energetic atmosphere in a historic King Street setting (Official Website)', 'Expansive cocktail program featuring gin and tonics on tap (Charleston Magazine)', 'Regularly featured in major publications like Bon Appétit, Southern Living, and Vogue (Official Website)']
attractions.tips: The restaurant operates on a first-come, first-serve basis and does not take reservations; it is highly recommended to arrive early to secure a spot (Official Website, Whiskey4Breakfast). If there is a wait, you can enjoy a drink at their nearby sister cocktail bar, Bar 167 (Official Website).
attractions.nearby: Bar 167, 167 Sushi Bar, Historic King Street Shopping District, Charleston City Market (Official Website, Tripadvisor)

attractions.name: Avery Research Center for African American History and Culture
attractions.description: The Avery Research Center for African American History and Culture is a division of the College of Charleston library system dedicated to collecting, preserving, and promoting the unique history of the African diaspora. It is housed in the historic Avery Normal Institute building, which served as the first accredited secondary school for African Americans in the South Carolina Lowcountry from 1865 to 1954 (Official Website, Wikipedia).
attractions.hours: Monday–Friday: 10:00 a.m. – 12:30 p.m., 1:30 p.m. – 4:30 p.m. (Official Website)
attractions.admission: Free (donations are welcomed) (Official Website)
highlights: ['Historic Avery Normal Institute building (1868) which served as a hub for Black leadership and education.', 'Extensive archives featuring over 6,000 primary and secondary source materials, including rare manuscripts and photographs.', 'Exhibits on Gullah Geechee culture and the history of slavery in the Lowcountry.', 'A restored historic classroom featuring period artifacts like a pot-bellied stove.', 'Sweetgrass basket collection and various West African material culture artifacts.', 'Rotating art exhibitions showcasing the work of South Carolina and African diaspora artists.']
attractions.tips: Self-guided tours are available on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays (advance registration is requested); archives access is by appointment only following a consultation. Limited visitor parking is available onsite, but visitors must obtain a permit from the building's office upon arrival (Official Website).
attractions.nearby: College of Charleston, Philip Simmons Garden, Broad Street Historic District, International African American Museum (Tripadvisor)

attractions.name: Boone Hall Plantation & Gardens (Boone Hall Plantation)
attractions.description: Boone Hall Plantation is one of America's oldest working plantations, continually producing agricultural crops for over 320 years. The site features a 1936 Colonial Revival-style dwelling, historic brick slave cabins, and the world-famous Avenue of Oaks. Visitors can explore the grounds, gardens, and exhibits that detail the plantation's rich and complex history (Boone Hall Plantation) (Wikipedia).
attractions.address: 1235 Long Point Rd, Mount Pleasant, SC 29464-9020
attractions.hours: Monday - Saturday: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM; Sunday: 12:00 PM - 5:00 PM (FareHarbor)
attractions.days_open: Open daily (Monday-Sunday), closed Thanksgiving Day and Christmas Day (Boone Hall Plantation)
attractions.admission: Adults (13-64): $30, Seniors (65+): $27, Children (6-12): $12, Military/AAA: $27, Children under 5: Free (Boone Hall Plantation) (FareHarbor)
highlights: 'World-famous Avenue of Oaks, a natural corridor of moss-draped live oak trees planted in 1743 ([Boone Hall Plantation)', "Nine original brick slave cabins featuring the 'Black History In America' exhibit (FareHarbor)", "Guided tours of the 1936 Colonial Revival mansion's first floor (Boone Hall Plantation)", "'Exploring The Gullah Culture' live presentations and history talks (Boone Hall Plantation)", 'Tractor-pulled covered wagon tours across the 738-acre property (FareHarbor)', 'Beautiful showcase gardens and marsh views with local wildlife (FareHarbor)']
attractions.tips: Arrive early to schedule your mansion tour time at the hospitality center as they can fill up. Note that photography is prohibited inside the house. Consider visiting during seasonal events like the Strawberry Festival or Pumpkin Patch for a different farm experience (Boone Hall Plantation) (Discover South Carolina).
attractions.nearby: USS Yorktown at Patriots Point, Shem Creek restaurants and boardwalks, Mount Pleasant Waterfront Park, and Boone Hall Farms Market (Tripadvisor) (Facebook)

attractions.name: Bulldog Tours (Official Website)
attractions.description: Bulldog Tours is Charleston's premier walking tour company, offering a variety of history, culinary, and ghost tours led by licensed, professional guides. They are the only company with exclusive nighttime access to many of the city's most haunted and historic landmarks, including the Old City Jail and Provost Dungeon. (Official Website, Tripadvisor)
attractions.hours: Daily: 8:00 AM – 10:00 PM (Apple Maps). Tour times typically range from 7:00 PM to 10:00 PM for ghost tours. (Charleston.com)
attractions.admission: Adults: $37 - $47+; Children (4-12): $27 - $37; Wee Ones (3 and under): FREE. Private tours start at $500. (Official Website, Charleston-SC.com)
highlights: "Exclusive nighttime access to the Old City Jail, Provost Dungeon, and Charleston's oldest graveyards ([Official Website)", 'Certified professional guides through the Palmetto Guild (Official Website)', 'Voted \\"Charleston\'s Best Tour Company\\" for over 14 consecutive years (Official Website)', 'Invests a portion of ticket proceeds back into the preservation of local historic landmarks (Official Website)', 'Offers specialized tours including the Dark Side of Charleston (Rated R) and the USS Yorktown Ghost Tour (Official Website)', 'Sustainable tour model that has raised over $6.9 million for preservation (Official Website)']
attractions.tips: Book directly on the official website for the lowest price guarantee. Wear comfortable walking shoes as tours involve significant walking on historic streets. Tours run rain or shine. (Official Website)
attractions.nearby: Charleston City Market, French Quarter, Waterfront Park, Old Exchange & Provost Dungeon, Philadelphia Alley (Tripadvisor, Official Website)

attractions.name: Cannonborough-Elliotborough Neighborhood
attractions.description: Cannonborough-Elliotborough is a vibrant, historic neighborhood in downtown Charleston known for its eclectic dining, unique boutiques, and charming, walkable streets. Often called the "gateway to the city," it features a rich mix of 19th-century architecture, including traditional Charleston single houses and historic Freedman's cottages. (Apartments.com, Living in Historic Charleston)
attractions.address: Bounded by Septima Clark Parkway, King St, Bee St, and President St (Representative: 134 Line St)
attractions.hours: Neighborhood: 24/7; Business/Park hours typically 8:00 AM – 10:00 PM (Airial Travel)
attractions.admission: Free (Airial Travel)
highlights: 'Eclectic culinary scene featuring local favorites like Chubby Fish, Malagón, and Pink Cactus ([Airial Travel)', 'Unique boutique shopping along King Street and charming side streets (AFAR)', "Historic architecture including restored single houses and rare post-Civil War Freedman's cottages (Living in Historic Charleston)", 'Elliotborough Park and the city’s first community garden (Apartments.com)', 'Walkable residential streets with a dynamic, youthful energy near MUSC and the College of Charleston (Pam Harrington Exclusives)']
attractions.tips: Explore the neighborhood on foot to discover hidden gems; book dinner reservations in advance for popular restaurants; visit during 'golden hours' for the best photography of the colorful historic buildings. (Airial Travel)
attractions.nearby: Upper King Street, Marion Square, MUSC, College of Charleston, Charleston Visitor Center (The Pinch)

attractions.name: Charleston Distilling Co.
attractions.description: Charleston Distilling Co. is an independent, small-batch distillery on Johns Island that crafts premium spirits using a meticulous farm-to-bottle approach. They manage every stage of production—from milling locally sourced grains to bottling—on-site to ensure the highest quality in their award-winning whiskeys, gins, and vodkas (Charleston Distilling Co.).
attractions.hours: Monday: 5:00 PM – 9:00 PM; Tuesday: Closed; Wednesday: Closed; Thursday: 5:00 PM – 9:00 PM; Friday: 5:00 PM – 10:00 PM; Saturday: 3:00 PM – 10:00 PM; Sunday: 3:00 PM – 9:00 PM (Charleston Distilling Co.)
attractions.days_open: Monday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday (Charleston Distilling Co.)
attractions.admission: Distillery Tours: $10 (over 18); Children: Free; Tasting Flights: $14–$16; Specialty Cocktails: $13 (Charleston Distilling Co., Charleston Distilling Co.)

attractions.tips: It is recommended to book distillery tours at least 24 hours in advance to guarantee a spot (Charleston Distilling Co.). Visitors should check the official website or social media for private event closures before planning a trip, as the venue is popular for weddings and parties (Charleston Distilling Co.).

Located in the heart of the Historic District at Marion Square, the market features a bounty of local produce, prepared food vendors, live entertainment, and a juried selection of arts and crafts from Lowcountry artisans Charleston Farmers Market.

attractions.name: Charleston Historic District (Charleston Old and Historic District)
attractions.description: The Charleston Historic District, also known as the Charleston Old and Historic District, is a National Historic Landmark District that serves as a living museum of America’s colonial and antebellum past. It covers much of the city's peninsular heart, featuring an unparalleled collection of 18th and 19th-century architecture, including its famous 'single houses' and cobblestone streets. This district was the first in the United States to be protected by local historic preservation legislation in 1931 (Wikipedia, City of Charleston).
attractions.hours: Public areas and streets: Open 24/7; Charleston Visitor Center: Daily 8:30 AM – 5:00 PM (Closed Thanksgiving, Christmas, and New Year's Day) (City of Charleston)
attractions.admission: Free (Public landmarks and streets); Paid admission for individual museums, historic house tours, and carriage rides (City of Charleston, NPS)
highlights: 'Rainbow Row: A series of thirteen pastel-colored Georgian-style row houses on East Bay Street ([Old Town Trolley)', 'The Battery & White Point Garden: A historic defensive seawall and promenade offering views of Charleston Harbor and Fort Sumter (NPS)', "Charleston City Market: One of the nation's oldest public markets, famous for its hand-woven Gullah sweetgrass baskets (City Market)", "St. Michael's Episcopal Church: The oldest surviving church building in Charleston, featuring an elegant Georgian design and historic clock (NPS)", 'Museum Mile: A section of Meeting Street home to numerous museums, historic houses, and cultural landmarks (Top Ten Travel List)']
attractions.tips: The district is best explored on foot to appreciate the intricate architectural details and hidden gardens. Utilize the free Downtown Area Shuttle (DASH) trolleys to travel between major landmarks without worrying about parking. Visit during spring (March–May) or fall (September–November) for the most comfortable weather and blooming gardens (Old Town Trolley).
attractions.nearby: South Carolina Aquarium, Waterfront Park, Fort Sumter, French Quarter, and College of Charleston campus (AFAR)

attractions.name: Charleston Stage
attractions.description: Charleston Stage is South Carolina’s largest professional theatre company and a 501(c)3 non-profit organization. Resident at the historic Dock Street Theatre since 1978, it produces over 120 performances annually ranging from traditional musicals to original plays and family-friendly adaptations. Source, Wikipedia
attractions.hours: Box Office: Tuesday - Friday 1:00 PM - 5:00 PM; Saturday 1:00 PM - 5:00 PM (on show days only). Performance times vary. Source
attractions.days_open: Tuesday - Saturday (Box Office); Performance days vary by season Source

Family Series: Adult/Senior $35.25, Student/Military $20.25. Prices include taxes and fees. PNC Pay-As-You-Please performances available for select shows. Source, Source
highlights: 'Resident professional theatre at the historic Dock Street Theatre, the first building in America built exclusively for theatrical performances.', 'Produces over 120 performances each season, reaching more than 40,000 patrons annually.', 'Offers extensive education programs including professional actor apprenticeships and acting classes for over 500 students.', 'Features a diverse lineup of MainStage musicals, plays, and a dedicated Family Series.', 'Known for original adaptations and world premieres, such as works by founder Julian Wiles.', 'Maintains a professional resident acting company and technical staff. [Source']
attractions.tips: Parking near the theater is restricted; use the nearby Cumberland Street or East Bay Street garages. attractions.description: The Charleston Symphony Orchestra is South Carolina's largest resident orchestra, performing over 150 events annually including its renowned Masterworks and Pops series. For more than 70 years, the organization has enriched the cultural life of the Lowcountry through exceptional symphonic music and extensive educational outreach programs.

attractions.description: The Children's Museum of the Lowcountry is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit, community-based organization dedicated to creating innovative learning experiences that equip and inspire children to become creative problem solvers. Established in 1995 and opened in 2003, the museum engages young children's potential by inviting families of all backgrounds to explore interactive environments. Official Website, Tripadvisor

attractions.name: Circular Congregational Church
attractions.description: Founded in 1681, the Circular Congregational Church is one of the oldest continuously worshipping congregations in the South and a National Historic Landmark. Its current Romanesque Revival structure, completed in 1892, features a unique circular exterior and a cloverleaf-shaped interior, while its historic graveyard contains monuments dating back to 1695. Circular Church, Wikipedia
attractions.hours: Church Interior: Monday-Friday 8:30 AM - 12:00 PM (subject to guide availability); Sunday Worship: 11:00 AM; Graveyard: Daily during daylight hours. Charleston-SC.com, Discover South Carolina
attractions.days_open: Daily (Church interior typically Monday-Friday; Graveyard daily) Charleston-SC.com, Ghost City Tours
attractions.admission: Free (donations appreciated); special events like "The Sound of Charleston" concerts may have fees (approx. $16-$35). Circular Church, Sound of Charleston
highlights: ['One of the oldest continuously worshipping congregations in the South, established in 1681.', 'The historic graveyard is likely the oldest English burial ground still in existence in Charleston, with markers from 1695.', 'The church building is a National Historic Landmark featuring a unique Romanesque Revival design with a circular footprint.', 'The Parish House was designed by Robert Mills, the architect of the Washington Monument.', "Home to 'The Sound of Charleston' concert series, featuring spirituals, gospel, and Gershwin.", "The sanctuary and graveyard are featured on numerous historical and ghost tours of Charleston's French Quarter."]
attractions.tips: Sunday morning visitors can find free parking until noon at the Cumberland Street Parking Garage. While the graveyard is open daily, access to the church interior is best found on weekday mornings when volunteer guides are present. Respectful behavior is required in the graveyard, which is still an active sacred site. Circular Church, Ghost City Tours

It features six miles of wide sandy beaches, a 1,045-foot fishing pier, and a charming downtown area filled with eclectic shops and seafood restaurants (Visit Folly, Wikipedia).

attractions.name: College of Charleston
attractions.description: The College of Charleston is a nationally recognized public liberal arts and sciences university located in the heart of historic Charleston, South Carolina. Founded in 1770 and chartered in 1785, it is the oldest university in South Carolina and the 13th-oldest institution of higher learning in the United States (Wikipedia).
attractions.hours: Visitor Center/Admissions: Monday - Friday 8:30 AM - 5:00 PM; Mace Brown Museum: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun 11:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Wednesday Closed (Official Site, Museum)
attractions.days_open: Monday - Sunday (Campus access); Monday - Friday (Admissions Office); Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun (Museum) (Official Site, Admissions)
attractions.admission: Free (Official Site, Yelp)
highlights: ['Randolph Hall (Historic oldest building)', 'The Cistern (Central campus landmark and gathering spot)', "Porter's Lodge (Iconic archway for convocation)", 'Mace Brown Museum of Natural History (Over 30,000 fossils)', 'Addlestone Library', 'Historic Harleston Village architecture']
attractions.tips: Wear comfortable walking shoes for navigating historic brick pathways, and schedule a student-led tour in advance for the best experience. Be sure to check out the Mace Brown Museum on the second floor of the School of Natural and Environmental Sciences building (Official Site, Today at CofC).

attractions.name: Cypress Gardens
attractions.description: Cypress Gardens is a 170-acre nature preserve and garden in South Carolina's Lowcountry, featuring an 80-acre blackwater bald cypress and tupelo swamp. Opened to the public in 1932, it is renowned for its scenic boat tours, diverse wildlife exhibits, and role as a filming location for iconic movies like The Notebook and The Patriot. Official Site, South Carolina Picture Project
attractions.hours: Daily: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM (Last admission at 4:00 PM; self-guided boat tours close at 4:15 PM) Official Site
attractions.days_open: Daily (Closed Thanksgiving, Christmas Eve, Christmas Day, and New Year's Day) Official Site

attractions.tips: Arrive early (near 9:00 AM) to secure a boat for tours and avoid the largest crowds. Note that pets are only permitted from November through February and must remain leashed. Wear comfortable shoes for the extensive trail system and check the seasonal bloom calendar for peak azalea and camellia viewing. Tripadvisor, Official Site

attractions.name: Edisto Beach State Park
attractions.description: Edisto Beach State Park is a 1,255-acre oceanfront destination rich in Native American history and natural beauty. It features South Carolina's longest system of ADA-accessible hiking and biking trails, which wind through maritime forests to a 4,000-year-old shell midden. Visitors can enjoy 1.5 miles of shell-laden beach, an interactive Environmental Learning Center, and varied overnight accommodations including saltmarsh cabins. South Carolina Parks, Wikipedia

attractions.hours: Park: 7 a.m. – 6 p.m. daily (extended to 8 p.m. during Daylight Savings Time); Office: M-F 9 a.m. – 5 p.m.; Environmental Learning Center: Tuesday-Saturday 9 a.m. – 4 p.m. South Carolina Parks

Helmed by James Beard Award-winning chefs, the bistro offers a regional menu with honest, simple preparations served in a convivial and approachable atmosphere. attractions.name: First Baptist Church of Charleston
attractions.description: First Baptist Church of Charleston is the oldest Baptist congregation in the American South, originally organized in 1682 and relocated to Charleston in 1696 (Official Site, Wikipedia)). The current historic sanctuary is a Greek Revival masterpiece designed by Robert Mills, the first American-born professional architect, and was completed in 1822 (Official Site).

attractions.days_open: Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday
attractions.admission: Free

attractions.tips: Visitors can utilize the church's rare downtown parking lot at 48 Meeting Street when attending services or scheduled tours (Official Site). For those interested in genealogy, the church has digitized early 19th-century membership books and burial lists available on their website (Official Site).

attractions.name: Fort Sumter and Fort Moultrie National Historical Park
attractions.description: Fort Sumter is a historic sea fort located at the entrance of Charleston Harbor, best known as the site where the first shots of the American Civil War were fired on April 12, 1861. Today, the National Park Service preserves the fort's ruins and offers educational exhibits that interpret the conflict's origins and its profound impact on American history.

attractions.days_open: Daily except for New Year's Day, Thanksgiving Day, and Christmas Day NPS Hours
attractions.admission: Fort Sumter entry is free, but ferry transportation is required. Ferry tickets via Fort Sumter Tours: Adults $43.00, Seniors/Active Military $39.00, Children (4-11) $26.00, Children 3 and under Free. NPS Fees
highlights: 'Site of the first shots of the American Civil War (April 12, 1861) [NPS History', 'The original 33-star U.S. flag that flew during the first bombardment Wikipedia', 'Well-preserved brick fort ruins and historic artillery displays NPS', "Museum exhibits detailing the causes of the Civil War and the fort's construction NPS Education", 'Scenic 30-minute ferry cruise across Charleston Harbor Fort Sumter Tours']
attractions.tips: Purchase ferry tickets in advance as they often sell out, especially during peak season. Arrive at the departure point at least 30 minutes before your scheduled ferry. Note that the fort is a trash-free facility, so visitors must pack out all waste. Visitor Safety

attractions.name: French Quarter District (Charleston's French Quarter)
attractions.description: The French Quarter is a historic neighborhood in downtown Charleston, celebrated for its well-preserved 18th and 19th-century architecture and romantic cobblestone streets. Named in 1973 to honor the area's history of French Huguenot merchants, the district is now a vibrant cultural hub home to numerous fine art galleries, historic churches, and iconic landmarks like Waterfront Park.
attractions.address: Bounded by Market St (North), Broad St (South), Meeting St (West), and Cooper River (East)
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours (Neighborhood); attractions typically open daily 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free (Public neighborhood); individual attraction fees vary
highlights: ["St. Philip's Episcopal Church and its historic graveyard", 'French Huguenot Church, featuring beautiful Gothic Revival architecture', 'Dock Street Theatre, known as the first building in America dedicated to theatrical performances', "The Pink House, one of Charleston's oldest masonry structures dating back to 1712", 'Waterfront Park and its iconic Pineapple Fountain overlooking Charleston Harbor', "Old Slave Mart Museum, which documents the city's complex history in the slave trade"]
attractions.tips: Wear comfortable walking shoes to navigate the historic cobblestone streets, particularly on Chalmers Street. Visit on a 'First Friday' of the month (excluding January and July) to experience the lively ArtWalk hosted by local galleries. A horse-drawn carriage tour is recommended for a relaxing overview of the neighborhood's deep history.

attractions.name: Gibbes Museum of Art
attractions.description: The Gibbes Museum of Art is home to a premier collection of American art that tells the story of Charleston and the American South. Located in a historic Beaux Arts building, the museum features over 350 years of artistic history, including paintings, sculptures, and a world-renowned collection of miniature portraits. Gibbes Museum of Art

attractions.name: Harleston Village
attractions.category: Neighborhood / Historic District (Harleston Village Association, Walks of Charleston)
attractions.description: Harleston Village is one of Charleston's oldest and most charming neighborhoods, dating back to 1672. It is characterized by its well-preserved 18th-century architecture, tree-lined streets, and iconic green spaces like Colonial Lake, blending a peaceful residential atmosphere with the lively energy of the College of Charleston. (Harleston Village Association, Walks of Charleston)

attractions.tips: Harleston Village is best explored on foot; wear comfortable shoes to enjoy the historic side streets. Visit Colonial Lake during the early morning or late afternoon golden hours for the best photography light. Stop by local favorites like Alcove Market or Second State Coffee for a break during your stroll, and remember to be respectful of the local residents. (Airial Travel, Harleston Village Association)

attractions.name: Heyward-Washington House
attractions.description: Built in 1772, the Heyward-Washington House was the town home of Thomas Heyward, Jr., a signer of the Declaration of Independence and a Revolutionary War patriot. The Georgian-style double house is most famous for hosting President George Washington during his week-long stay in May 1791, leading to its traditional name. It was acquired by the Charleston Museum in 1929 and opened the following year as the city’s first historic house museum. National Park Service, Official Website
attractions.hours: Monday - Saturday: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM; Sunday: 12:00 PM – 5:00 PM (Last tour typically begins at 4:15 PM). Official Website
attractions.admission: Adults (18+): $15, Youth (13-17): $12, Children (3-12): $6, Children 2 and under: Free. Combo tickets for 2 or 3 sites (including The Charleston Museum and Joseph Manigault House) are available for additional savings. Official Tickets
highlights: ['Home of Thomas Heyward Jr., a signer of the Declaration of Independence.', 'Hosted President George Washington during his 1791 Southern tour.', 'Features the "Holmes Bookcase," considered one of the finest examples of American-made colonial furniture.', 'Includes the only 1740s kitchen building open to the public in Charleston.', 'Beautifully maintained formal knot garden designed to be historically accurate to the late 1700s.', 'Recognized as a National Historic Landmark since 1970.']
attractions.tips: Visitors can choose between a standard audio tour focusing on the collections and a Revolutionary War-themed audio tour. Purchasing a combo ticket with the Charleston Museum and the Joseph Manigault House offers significant savings. Be aware that touring the house requires climbing stairs, though photographic tours are available for those with limited mobility. The property also features a modernized outdoor privy, as there are no public restroom facilities inside the historic house. TripAdvisor, Official Website
attractions.nearby: St. Michael's Church, Rainbow Row, Nathaniel Russell House, Old Exchange & Provost Dungeon, and The Battery. TripAdvisor

attractions.name: High Wire Distilling Co.
attractions.description: High Wire Distilling Co. is a small-batch distillery in downtown Charleston that specializes in reviving forgotten heritage grains and restoring historic flavors to modern spirits. Founded by Scott Blackwell and Ann Marshall, the distillery is renowned for its Jimmy Red Bourbon, crafted from a rare magenta-hued corn once nearly extinct. (High Wire Distilling)
attractions.hours: Bottle Sales: Mon-Sat 10:00 AM - 7:00 PM; Bar Hours: Mon-Thurs 10:00 AM - 7:00 PM, Fri-Sat 10:00 AM - 9:00 PM; Tours: Thurs-Sat at 12:00 PM, 2:00 PM, 4:00 PM, and 6:00 PM. (High Wire Distilling)
attractions.admission: Tours: $10 per person (Reservations strongly recommended; tastings not included). Cocktail Classes: $75 per person / $130 per couple. Bar/Tasting Room: Entry is typically free; spirits and cocktails are available for purchase. (High Wire Distilling)
highlights: ['Produces spirits using heritage grains sourced from South Carolina family farms.', 'Home of the famous Jimmy Red Straight Bourbon Whiskey, made from 100% heritage Jimmy Red corn.', 'Features a 3,200 square foot event space with a 16-foot high barrel wall and production views.', 'Offers guided tours that cover the history of the distillery and the "seed to bottle" production process.', 'Host of interactive cocktail classes where guests learn to balance botanical flavors and craft seasonal drinks.', 'The first distillery to operate in downtown Charleston since the Prohibition era.']
attractions.tips: Reservations for tours are strongly encouraged as they fill up quickly. If you're looking for a more personalized experience, inquire about their private tours and tastings. Don't miss the chance to try a cocktail made with their flagship Jimmy Red bourbon while overlooking the production area. (High Wire Distilling)

attractions.name: Husk Charleston

attractions.name: International African American Museum (IAAM)
attractions.description: The International African American Museum is a groundbreaking institution located at the historic Gadsden’s Wharf, one of the primary points of entry for enslaved Africans in North America. It tells the powerful stories of the African American journey through transformative storytelling, artifacts, and immersive exhibitions that highlight labor, resistance, and ingenuity. (Wikipedia, Official Website)

attractions.name: Isle of Palms

Located just 15 miles from downtown Charleston, it offers a blend of residential charm, upscale resort amenities at Wild Dunes, and diverse outdoor activities including world-class golf and water sports. City of Isle of Palms, Wikipedia

attractions.name: Joseph P. Riley Jr. Park
attractions.description: Opened in 1997, Joseph P. Riley Jr. Park is a premier minor league baseball stadium located on the banks of the Ashley River in Charleston, South Carolina (MiLB). Nicknamed 'The Joe' after the city's longest-serving mayor, the stadium serves as the home field for the Charleston RiverDogs and The Citadel Bulldogs baseball teams (Wikipedia). It is renowned for its boisterous and irreverent atmosphere, famously influenced by co-owner and 'Director of Fun' Bill Murray (MLB.com).

attractions.name: King Street Shopping District
attractions.description: King Street is Charleston's premier historic thoroughfare and one of the most vibrant shopping districts in the United States. Named after King Charles II, this 300-year-old street is divided into three distinct areas: the Lower King Antique District, the Middle King Fashion District, and the Upper King Design and Dining District. It offers a unique blend of high-end national retailers, locally owned boutiques, art galleries, and acclaimed restaurants housed in beautifully preserved architecture.

attractions.name: Marion Square
attractions.description: Marion Square is a historic 10-acre public park in the heart of downtown Charleston that originally served as a parade ground for The Citadel. It now serves as a central hub for community life, hosting major events like the Charleston Farmers Market and various arts and food festivals. The square features wide-open green spaces popular for sunbathing and picnics, as well as several significant historical monuments and a colonial-era defensive wall remnant.

attractions.admission: Free
highlights: ['Hosts the Charleston Farmers Market every Saturday from April through November.', 'Home to significant monuments, including a Holocaust Memorial and the Wade Hampton III obelisk.', "Features a remnant of the city's 18th-century defensive hornwork wall.", 'Served as the original parade ground for the South Carolina Military Academy (The Citadel).', 'A central venue for major city events like Spoleto Festival USA and the Charleston Food and Wine Festival.', 'Bordered by iconic landmarks like the Francis Marion Hotel and the Old Citadel building.']
attractions.tips: Visit on Saturday mornings to enjoy the Farmers Market's local produce and crafts. The park offers free public Wi-Fi and is a great spot for a picnic, but be sure to bring a blanket as seating is limited in the open field. Check the local event calendar for seasonal holiday markets and festivals that often take place here.

The site offers an inclusive history of the Middleton family and the enslaved people who lived and worked there, featuring a House Museum, Stableyards, and over 65 acres of formal gardens (Middleton Place).

attractions.tips: Wear comfortable walking shoes for the extensive outdoor paths and bring insect repellent. Check the daily schedule at the Visitor Center upon arrival for tour times. Picnics are permitted on the Greensward with paid admission (Middleton Place Visitor Tips).

The current Gothic Revival structure, completed in 1891, serves as a monumental landmark of African American history and a symbol of hope following the 2015 Emanuel Nine tragedy.

Founded by Carrie Morey, these small shops offer a cozy atmosphere where visitors can enjoy traditional buttermilk biscuits, savory ham-filled treats, and sweet varieties like blackberry or cinnamon. The shop has gained national acclaim, appearing on Oprah's Favorite Things and being featured in numerous culinary publications.

attractions.name: St. Philip's Church
attractions.description: Established in 1680, St. Philip's is the oldest religious congregation in South Carolina and houses the first Anglican congregation south of Virginia. The current stuccoed brick building was constructed in 1836 and features an imposing 200-foot steeple designed in the Wren-Gibbs tradition, which once served as a navigational lighthouse for Charleston Harbor. As a National Historic Landmark, it remains a vibrant center of faith and a cornerstone of the city's historic French Quarter. (stpchurch.org, NPS)

attractions.name: The Pink House (The Pink House Gallery) (Wikipedia))
attractions.description: The Pink House is one of the oldest buildings in Charleston and South Carolina, constructed between 1694 and 1712 from unique pink Bermuda stone (Wikipedia)). This three-story structure has a diverse history, having served as a colonial tavern, a brothel, a law office, and an art studio for famed local artists such as Alice Ravenel Huger Smith (SC Picture Project). Today, it remains a beloved historic landmark and art gallery, easily recognizable by its salmon hue and gambrel tile roof (Charleston.com).

attractions.tips: The building is one of the most photographed in Charleston; late afternoon light highlights the pink stone best (Tripadvisor). attractions.name: Charles Towne Landing State Historic Site
attractions.description: Charles Towne Landing State Historic Site preserves the original site of the first permanent English settlement in Carolina, established in 1670. The 664-acre park introduces visitors to early colonial history through interactive museum exhibits, a self-guided history trail, and archaeological excavations. It features unique attractions like a 17th-century replica sailing ship and a natural habitat zoo showcasing animals from the colonial era. Official Site, Wikipedia
attractions.hours: Park Grounds: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM daily; Visitor Center/Gift Shop: 9:00 AM - 4:00 PM daily; Animal Forest: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM daily; Adventure ship: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM daily (Closed Tuesdays) Official Site
attractions.admission: $12.00 per adult; $7.50 for S.C. Seniors; $7.00 for youth (age 6-15); Free for children 5 and younger Official Site
highlights: ['Adventure, a full-scale replica 17th-century trading ketch docked in Old Towne Creek', 'Animal Forest, a 22-acre natural habitat zoo featuring bears, bison, and otters', '80 acres of historic gardens and a majestic live oak alley dating back centuries', '12-room interactive museum in the Visitor Center with hands-on colonial exhibits', 'Reconstructed earthwork fortifications and six fireable replica cannons', 'Seven miles of interpretive trails for walking and biking throughout the historic grounds']
attractions.tips: Wear comfortable walking shoes as the site covers over 600 acres with several miles of trails. Rent a bike at the visitor center to cover the grounds more quickly. Note that the replica ship 'Adventure' is closed on Tuesdays and away for maintenance from January 1 through April 1 each year. Official Site, Yelp

It is renowned for its "Romantic-style" gardens, ancient live oaks draped in Spanish moss, and its commitment to preserving the complex history of the South through guided tours of its historic house and preserved slave cabins (Magnolia Plantation Official Site, Traveler of Charleston).

The site stands as a powerful place of remembrance and learning, honoring the lives, resilience, and lasting cultural influence of the enslaved people who lived and worked there (Charleston County Parks).

attractions.category: National Historical Park, Landmark, Museum Wikipedia
attractions.description: Fort Moultrie is a historic series of fortifications on Sullivan's Island that protected Charleston Harbor for 171 years, from the American Revolution through World War II. Originally built of palmetto logs and sand, it was the site of the first decisive American victory over the British Navy in 1776. Today, the park features restored sections representing different eras of coastal defense history NPS, American Battlefield Trust.

attractions.hours: Daily: 9:00 am – 4:30 pm (Grounds, Visitor Center, and Restrooms) NPS
